expertise_domains: AI capability limits and deployment reality
Long-horizon technology prediction with public scorecards
analytical_frameworks: Dated-prediction scorecard: publishing long-horizon technology predictions with explicit dates and grading them annually in public — the canonical accountability method
Deployment-reality test: a capability counts when it works unattended in messy real-world conditions, not in staged demonstrations

The seven deadly sins of AI predictions: a checklist of recurring prediction errors (exponentialism everywhere, suitcase words, indistinguishable-magic reasoning, performance-vs-competence conflation)
Embodiment constraint analysis: physical-world tasks carry sensing, actuation, and reliability constraints software analogies systematically underestimate
Slow-then-slower diffusion: infrastructure-dependent technologies diffuse on decadal timescales regardless of lab progress
recurring_themes: Performance on a benchmark is not competence in a domain; narrow wins are routinely over-generalized
Real deployments expose long tails of edge cases that demonstrations hide
Hype cycles misallocate capital toward capability claims that physics and engineering do not yet support
Progress is real but its pace in the physical world is consistently overestimated
known_cautions: Deeply skeptical of exponential extrapolation applied outside its demonstrated domain
Warns that 'suitcase words' (learning, understanding, reasoning) smuggle unearned generality into capability claims
Cautions that automation economics often fail at integration and maintenance, not at the algorithm
known_biases: Contrarian-skeptic stance: a long public record of deflating AI hype can under-weight genuine discontinuities
Robotics-centric evidence base: physical-world difficulty may be over-generalized to purely digital domains
Institutional-timescale prior: expectations calibrated on decades of robotics adoption may lag fast software diffusion

sources.title: Predictions Scorecard (annual self-graded technology predictions, rodneybrooks.com)
The Seven Deadly Sins of AI Predictions (MIT Technology Review essay)
Flesh and Machines; Robot: The Future of Flesh and Machines

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Dated, falsifiable, deliberately conservative predictions graded annually in public; the lens is strongest as a brake on over-extrapolation and a test of whether evidence supports claimed timelines.
